claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You do not live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant does not live in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant does not live in the UK
circumstances: Claimant does not live in the UK
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start by gathering some information about your circumstances.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
To assess your eligibility for Child Benefit, I'll need to ask you some questions. Child Benefit is assessed on a per-child basis, so let me start with:

**Do you have a child or children that you're responsible for?** If yes, could you tell me how many children you're asking about?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I'm interested in claiming Child Benefit. I have one child - my son Alex, who's 8 years old. He lives with me, but I should mention that I don't currently live in the UK. Does that affect whether I can claim?

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Yes, your residency status is very important for Child Benefit eligibility.

You've mentioned that you don't currently live in the UK. To properly assess your eligibility, I need to understand your situation better:

**Are you currently living abroad temporarily, or have you moved abroad permanently?** 

Also, could you tell me:
- **Which country are you currently living in?**
- **Do you or your partner work for the UK government or certain international organizations?**

Your residency and connection to the UK will determine whether you can claim Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I've moved abroad permanently - I'm living in Spain now. And no, neither I nor my partner work for the UK government or any international organizations. We both work locally here in Spain.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. This is an important factor for your eligibility.

Let me ask you a few more questions to complete the assessment:

**Are you or your partner subject to UK taxation, or do either of you pay UK National Insurance contributions?** 

This can sometimes apply even when living abroad, particularly if you have certain employment arrangements or income from the UK.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, we're not subject to UK taxation and we don't pay UK National Insurance contributions. We pay all our taxes here in Spain now.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing that information. Let me ask one more important question:

**Are you or your partner covered by UK social security legislation through any reciprocal agreement or EU coordination rules?** 

For example, this might apply if you moved to Spain recently and are still covered under certain transitional arrangements, or if you have a specific situation that keeps you under UK social security rules.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, we're not covered by UK social security legislation. We're fully under the Spanish social security system now - no transitional arrangements or anything like that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. Let me gather the remaining details to complete the assessment for Alex.

**Could you confirm:**

1. **Does Alex live with you in Spain?**
2. **Is Alex working full-time (defined as 24 hours or more per week)?** (I assume not given he's only 8, but I need to confirm for the assessment)
3. **Are you responsible for Alex - meaning you have parental responsibility and provide for his care?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Yes, that's right:

1. Yes, Alex lives with me here in Spain
2. No, he's not working at all - he's only 8, he's at primary school
3. Yes, I'm his parent and I'm responsible for him - I provide for all his care and needs

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. I now have enough details to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

Let me carefully review the situation against the eligibility criteria:

**Your situation:**
- You have one child, Alex, who is 8 years old
- Alex lives with you in Spain
- You are responsible for Alex and provide for his care
- You have moved permanently to Spain
- You are not subject to UK taxation
- You do not pay UK National Insurance contributions
- You are not covered by UK social security legislation
- Neither you nor your partner work for the UK government or international organizations

**Assessment:**

Unfortunately, **you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex**.

**Reason:** 

Child Benefit has a residency requirement. To be eligible, you must either:
- Live in the UK, OR
- Be covered by UK social security legislation (through reciprocal agreements or EU coordination rules), OR
- Work for the UK government or certain international organizations abroad

Since you have permanently moved to Spain, are not covered by UK social security legislation, do not pay UK National Insurance, are not subject to UK taxation, and don't work for the UK government, you do not meet the residency requirements for Child Benefit.

While Alex meets the age criteria (being under 16 and not working full-time), and you meet the responsibility criteria (being his parent and providing care), the fact that you are permanently resident abroad and not connected to the UK social security system means you cannot claim UK Child Benefit.

**Note:** You may be eligible for equivalent family benefits under the Spanish social security system. I'd recommend checking with the Spanish authorities about child benefits available to residents in Spain.

Is there anything else you'd like me to clarify about this decision?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, that's clear. Thank you for explaining it all to me. I understand now that I can't claim Child Benefit because I don't live in the UK anymore. I'll look into what's available here in Spain instead.
